_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Butler County, PA. Formed 1800 from Allegheny County. County seat at Butler. Agricultural county with early iron furnaces on Slippery Rock Creek, later oil and gas development. Good record survival from county formation.

previous_courthouses.fate: Log courthouse. Butler County was carved from Allegheny County in a region still transitioning from frontier to settled agricultural land.
Frame and brick courthouse. Served the growing agricultural and early iron-producing county.
Brick courthouse. Served through the Civil War and the oil boom era. A fire in the courthouse on January 11, 1883, caused some record damage.

records_destroyed: loose case files in offices directly affected
records_survived: the bound record volumes were saved
record_losses.description: A fire on January 11, 1883, damaged the Butler County courthouse. The fire affected offices and some loose files, but the bound record books — deeds, wills, court minutes — were rescued. The loss was primarily to loose papers, case files, and office materials. The bound volumes that form the genealogical backbone of the records survived intact.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} had deeds, wills, or court records in Butler County, the bound volumes survived the 1883 fire. Some individual case files may have been lost, but the indexed volumes that genealogists rely on are intact from 1800.
record_losses.source: McKee, J.A. History of Butler County, Pennsylvania. Chicago: Waterman, Watkins & Co., 1895.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from county formation. Survived the 1883 fire.
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps in bound deed books. Pre-1800 deeds for the Butler area filed under Allegheny County.
deeds.repository: Butler County Recorder of Deeds; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Good — from county formation. Bound will books survived the fire.
wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose estate papers may have been affected by the 1883 fire.
wills_estates.repository: Butler County Register of Wills; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1885. Pre-1885 marriages through church records.
marriages.gaps: 85-year gap between county formation (1800) and marriage license law (1885) filled by church records. Presbyterian and Lutheran congregations were particularly strong in Butler County.
marriages.repository: Butler County Clerk of the Orphans' Court; Pennsylvania State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good — bound volumes continuous from formation
court_minutes.gaps: Some loose case files lost in the 1883 fire.
court_minutes.repository: Butler County Prothonotary; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

vital_records.repository: Pennsylvania Department of Health; Pennsylvania State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Butler County had early charcoal iron furnaces along Slippery Rock Creek and Connoquenessing Creek. Furnace account books and daybooks document workers, ore suppliers, and charcoal burners.
substitute_sources.repository: Butler County Historical Society; Pennsylvania State Archives; Hagley Museum and Library
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Iron furnace account books name workers (including woodcutters and colliers) and record wages, purchases, and dates of employment. These records document families who owned no land and appear in few county records.
substitute_sources.source: Bining, Arthur Cecil. Pennsylvania Iron Manufacture in the Eighteenth Century. Harrisburg: Pennsylvania Historical Commission, 1938.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1800–1940. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide detail on iron production and farming.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. Manufacturing schedules (1820–1880) document iron furnaces. Agricultural schedules show the farming economy that supported the rural portions of the county.

substitute_sources.description: Butler County's earliest congregations were Presbyterian (Scots-Irish) and Lutheran (German). Church records supplement civil records, particularly for marriages before 1885.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Presbyterian Historical Society; Evangelical Lutheran Church archives; Butler County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for pre-1885 marriages and for tracing the ethnic origins of settler families. German Lutheran records may be in German script for the earliest entries.
substitute_sources.source: Butler County Historical Society. Church Records Collection.

narrative_hooks: Butler County's courthouse fire in 1883 damaged some loose files, but the bound deed and will books survived — if {ancestor_name} owned land or left a will, the record is there from 1800.

If {ancestor_name} worked at one of Butler County's charcoal iron furnaces along Slippery Rock Creek, the furnace account books may name {pronoun_object} when the county records do not — furnace workers who owned no land left their traces in company daybooks.
Pre-1800 records for {ancestor_name}'s land in the Butler County area are filed under Allegheny County — the parent county whose records cover the earliest deeds.
Pennsylvania's marriage license law started in 1885 — if {ancestor_name} married in Butler County before that date, the Presbyterian or Lutheran church records are the first place to look.
source_references: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.
McKee, J.A. History of Butler County, Pennsylvania. Chicago: Waterman, Watkins & Co., 1895.
Bining, Arthur Cecil. Pennsylvania Iron Manufacture in the Eighteenth Century. Harrisburg: Pennsylvania Historical Commission, 1938.
